lesson.subtitle: Aula 9 -- Agreeing and Disagreeing -- Professional Debates
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Natalie Viegas | Aula 9 -- Agreeing and Disagreeing

listenings.text: Hi Natalie, this is Paul from the strategy team. On Thursday we have a team debate about our new project tool. Some people want to keep the current system, and others want to change it. I would like you to prepare your standpoint and present it in two minutes. Please give one clear argument and be ready for a counterargument. Remember, the goal is not to win; the goal is to reach a good decision together. Stay reasonable, listen to the other side, and look for common ground. If people raise an objection, concede the valid points and explain where you still disagree. A small compromise is often better than a long fight. Send me your main argument before the meeting. Thank you, and good luck.

Hi, this is Natalie, rehearsing my opening for the team debate. Here it is. Thank you all for being here. In my standpoint, we should change the project tool this quarter. The current system is familiar; however, it is slow and hard to scale. I understand the objection about training time. That is a valid point, and I concede it. On the other hand, the new tool saves hours every week, so the benefit is bigger than the cost. Although change is never easy, I believe we can find common ground. My proposal is a small pilot first. If it works, we move the whole team. I think that is a reasonable compromise, and I hope we can reach a consensus today. Thank you for listening.

extra_audio.text: First, you listen to the other standpoint with respect. Then you concede any valid points in their argument. After that, you state your objection with a clear reason. Next, you use a linking word like however to show the contrast. Finally, you offer a reasonable compromise to reach common ground.
